name: Data patterns at scale
description: Data at scale from the Dennemeyer 100: outbox, sharding and hot shards, replication and read-your-writes, optimistic locking, SQL performance (indexes, N+1, pools), caching and invalidation, and scaling fundamentals.

topics.name: Outbox pattern
topics.description: Solving the dual-write problem: persist state and publish events atomically via an outbox table.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

The outbox pattern is a reliable way to do two things that must happen together: save a change in the database AND publish an event about it — without losing either when things crash.

## What problem does it solve?

A service saves a renewal and must announce "RenewalCreated" to other systems. Database commit and message publish are two separate systems: if you crash between them, you either saved without announcing (others never learn about it) or announced something that was never saved. Distributed transactions across a DB and a message broker are impractical.

## How it works

Use ONE transaction in ONE system: in the same database transaction that saves the business data, also insert the event into an "outbox" table. The commit is atomic — either both rows exist or neither does.

A separate worker then reads the outbox table and publishes the events to the broker, marking rows as sent (either by polling or by change-data-capture).

The consequence to know: delivery becomes at-least-once. The worker may crash after publishing but before marking the row, so the same event can go out TWICE. Consumers must therefore be idempotent — typically a processed-events table keyed by eventId: "seen it, skipping". The pair works together: outbox guarantees the event goes out, idempotent consumers make duplicates harmless.

## Common mistakes

- Publishing to the broker inside the DB transaction (or after it) and losing events on crash
- Forgetting delivery becomes at-least-once — consumers must be idempotent
- No cleanup/monitoring on the outbox table; it grows forever

## Key terms

- Outbox table — events written in the same transaction as the data
- Atomic commit — both saved or neither
- At-least-once delivery — duplicates possible by design
- Idempotent consumer — processing twice has no double effect

topics.name: Sharding & partitioning
topics.description: Splitting data across nodes by key; choosing partition keys and handling hot shards.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Sharding (partitioning) means splitting one database's data across several databases/nodes — for example, customers A–M on one shard and N–Z on another — because a single node can no longer handle the data volume or traffic.

## What problem does it solve?

A single database has a ceiling: storage, connections, write throughput. When indexes, caching and read replicas are exhausted, splitting the DATA itself is what remains.

## How it works

The central decision is the shard key — the field that decides which shard a row lives on. A good key spreads load evenly AND matches your main queries (hash of customerId is the classic: even spread, and customer lookups know exactly where to go).

The failure mode is the hot shard: one shard getting a disproportionate share of traffic. Shard by country and 80% of traffic comes from one country; shard by tenant and one giant customer melts a shard alone. Fixes: hash-based keys, or splitting the big tenant further.

The costs that make sharding a LAST resort:
- Cross-shard queries: a query touching many shards must fan out to all of them and merge results — expensive.
- No cross-shard transactions: atomic updates across shards are gone.
- Rebalancing: adding a shard means moving data while live.

Interview framing: mention sharding as the escalation AFTER indexing, caching, and read replicas — not the first move.

## Common mistakes

- Sharding before exhausting indexes, caching and read replicas
- A shard key that matches queries but skews load (country, big tenant) → hot shard
- Designing as if cross-shard joins and transactions still existed

## Key terms

- Shard key — the field that routes rows to shards
- Hot shard — one shard overloaded by skewed data/traffic
- Fan-out query — asking every shard and merging results
- Rebalancing — redistributing data when shards are added

topics.name: Replication & read replicas
topics.description: Copies of data for read scaling and availability; replication lag trade-offs.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Database replication keeps live copies of the data on several servers: one primary takes all the WRITES, one or more replicas serve READS.

## What problem does it solve?

Two problems at once. Scale: most applications read far more than they write, so spreading reads over replicas multiplies capacity, and heavy dashboards/reports can read from a replica without slowing down transactions. Availability: when the primary dies, a replica can be promoted to take over.

## How it works

Writes go to the primary, which streams its changes to the replicas. Replication is usually asynchronous — and that creates replication lag: a freshly written row may take a moment to appear on a replica.

The classic bug this causes: the user saves a payment (write → primary), the next screen reads the status (read → replica), and the replica has not caught up yet — the user sees the OLD status right after a successful save.

The standard fix is read-your-own-writes: right after a write, read that data back from the PRIMARY; use replicas for everything that tolerates a few seconds of staleness (dashboards, lists, reports).

Keep the two purposes separate in an answer: read scaling (replicas share the read load) and failover (a replica is promoted when the primary fails) — related machinery, different stories.

## Common mistakes

- Reading your own write from a replica — the user sees pre-save data
- Treating read scaling and failover as the same story
- Ignoring replica lag in dashboards that claim to be real-time

## Key terms

- Primary / replica — writes vs reads
- Replication lag — the delay before a write appears on replicas
- Read-your-own-writes — read fresh data from the primary after writing
- Failover — promoting a replica when the primary dies

topics.name: Optimistic locking
topics.description: Detecting concurrent updates with versions instead of holding locks.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Optimistic locking is a way to handle two people editing the same record at once: instead of locking the record, you detect the conflict at SAVE time using a version number.

## What problem does it solve?

Two admins open the same record. Both edit. Both save. Without protection, the second save silently overwrites the first — the "lost update" problem. Nobody notices until the data is wrong.

## How it works

Each record carries a version number. When a client loads the record, it gets version 7. When it saves, the update says "set these fields WHERE version = 7, and bump to 8". If someone else saved in between, the row is already version 8, the update matches nothing, and the server answers 409 Conflict. The client then reloads the fresh data and reapplies its change deliberately — no silent overwrite.

It is called OPTIMISTIC because you assume conflicts are rare and pay only when they happen. The alternative, pessimistic locking, locks the record up front — nobody else can even open it for editing. That is safer under constant contention but terrible for user-facing flows, because locks get held while users think, go to lunch, or close the laptop.

This connects to transaction isolation levels: databases can also prevent anomalies with stronger isolation, at the cost of more locking. For long user-facing edit sessions — which cannot be wrapped in one DB transaction — the version check is the practical answer.

## Common mistakes

- No version check at all — the second save silently erases the first
- Pessimistic locks held across user think-time
- Retrying a 409 automatically with the stale data instead of reloading

## Key terms

- Lost update — the second save silently erasing the first
- Version column — the counter that detects concurrent edits
- 409 Conflict — the HTTP answer for "someone saved before you"
- Optimistic vs pessimistic — detect at save vs lock up front

topics.name: SQL Performance: Indexes, Transactions & Pools
topics.description: Indexing trade-offs, transactions and isolation, SQL vs NoSQL, the N+1 problem, connection pools.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

The handful of database concepts behind most slow-endpoint investigations: indexes, transactions and isolation, the SQL vs NoSQL choice, the N+1 problem, and connection pools.

## What problem does it solve?

"The list endpoint got slow as data grew" is one of the most common production issues — and one of the most common interview scenarios. These concepts are the diagnosis toolkit.

## How it works

Indexes: an index is a fast lookup path on chosen columns — like a book's index. Index the columns you filter, sort or join on (customerId, status, createdAt). Without one, the database scans the whole table. The trade-off: every index slows WRITES a little (it must be updated too) and takes space — so index deliberately, not everything.

Transactions: several operations succeed or fail as one unit — save the renewal AND its history entry, or neither. Isolation levels tune how much running transactions see of each other: stricter = safer but more locking; looser = faster but anomalies (dirty reads) appear. Frame it as a consistency-vs-performance dial.

SQL vs NoSQL: decide by ACCESS PATTERN. Relations, joins, transactions, strong consistency → SQL. Flexible schema, huge scale, document/log-shaped data, simple key lookups → NoSQL is a candidate. Never "NoSQL because it's modern".

N+1 problem: fetch a list with one query, then run one MORE query per row (load 100 renewals, then 100 customer lookups). Fix: a join, eager loading, or batch loading by id set.

Connection pool: opening a DB connection is expensive, so a pool reuses them. Too small → requests queue up; too large → the DB drowns. The multi-instance trap: pools ADD UP — 10 pods × 50 connections = 500 connections against one database. Size pools globally, not per instance.

## Common mistakes

- Indexing everything — writes pay for every index
- Missing the N+1 because each query is individually fast
- Per-pod connection pools sized in isolation (10 pods × 50 = 500 on one DB)
- Adding hardware before reading a single query plan

## Key terms

- Index — fast lookup path; speeds reads, taxes writes
- Query plan / EXPLAIN — how the DB actually executes your query
- N+1 — one query per row; fix with join/batch
- Connection pool — reused connections; sizes multiply across instances
- Isolation level — consistency vs performance dial

topics.name: Caching & Invalidation
topics.description: When to cache, the failure modes (stale data, stampede), and invalidation strategies.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Caching keeps frequently read data in a fast place (Redis, a CDN, memory) so you do not recompute or re-fetch it every time — plus the strategies for keeping the cache from serving stale data.

## What problem does it solve?

An expensive aggregate (a customer dashboard summary) computed on every request wastes the database. Caching answers most requests instantly — IF you manage the staleness honestly.

## How it works

Before caching anything, ask two questions: how often is this read, and how fresh must it be? A portfolio summary can be 5 minutes old; a payment status cannot. Caching the wrong thing shows customers wrong business data.

Invalidation — deciding when cached data must be refreshed — is the famous hard part. The options, usually combined:
- TTL (time-to-live): entries expire after N minutes. Simplest; "data may be N minutes old" becomes an explicit product decision.
- Explicit invalidation: when the underlying data changes, delete the affected cache keys. Precise, but you must find every key the change affects.
- Write-through: writes update the cache and the store together — always fresh, writes cost more.
- Background refresh: a job recomputes hot entries before they expire.

The failure mode with a name: cache stampede. A popular key expires, and a thousand simultaneous requests all miss and hit the database at once. Mitigations: staggered TTLs, or letting one request recompute while the rest briefly get the old value.

## Common mistakes

- Caching payment/critical status because "it's read often"
- No stampede protection on hot keys — expiry becomes a DB thundering herd
- Invalidation as an afterthought; users see stale business data
- TTL chosen by feel, not as an explicit staleness decision

## Key terms

- TTL — expiry time; staleness as an explicit decision
- Invalidation — removing/updating cache entries on change
- Write-through — update cache and store together
- Cache stampede — thundering herd on an expired hot key
- Stale data — the risk that makes cache design a product question

topics.name: Scaling Fundamentals
topics.description: Horizontal vs vertical, stateless services, latency vs throughput, percentiles, and finding the bottleneck.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

The basic vocabulary of making a system handle more load: vertical vs horizontal scaling, stateless services, latency vs throughput, percentiles, and bottlenecks.

## What problem does it solve?

"Traffic is growing" forces decisions. These concepts are the shared language for making them correctly instead of just "add more servers".

## How it works

Vertical scaling: a bigger machine. Simple, but has a ceiling and a single point of failure. Horizontal scaling: more instances/pods behind a load balancer — the cloud default, but it has a precondition: the service must be STATELESS. If any instance can serve any request (state lives in the DB, Redis, or a JWT), balancing is trivial. A WebSocket service is stateful — the connection is pinned to one instance — so it needs extra machinery (Redis pub/sub) to scale out.

Scalability vs elasticity: scalability = CAN handle more with more resources; elasticity = scales itself up AND down automatically (Kubernetes HPA: 3 pods → 10 under load → back to 3). Scaling down safely is a cost story worth mentioning.

The measurement vocabulary:
- Latency = one request's response time. Throughput = requests completed per second. They are independent: a system can push 5000 req/s while each request takes 2 seconds — great throughput, bad experience.
- Percentiles: averages hide suffering. Average 80ms with p99 = 3s means 1% of users wait three seconds — invisible in the mean. Set targets and alerts on p95/p99 (tail latency), not averages.
- Bottleneck: the actual limiting factor — a DB query, a missing index, the connection pool, CPU, an external API, lock contention. MEASURE first: if the database is the bottleneck, adding app pods makes things WORSE (more connections hammering the same DB).

## Sizing math (back of the envelope)

Turn big numbers into engineering quantities before designing. 100 million requests/month ÷ (30 × 86,400 s) ≈ 39 requests per second on AVERAGE — suddenly not scary. Then refine: peak can be 5–10× the average (size for peak); ask what a typical request COSTS (a cache hit vs a five-table join); and remember the database and external APIs cap you long before the app tier does.

The formula connecting speed and resources: concurrency ≈ rps × latency in seconds. 39 rps at 200ms ≈ 8 requests in flight — trivial. The same 39 rps with a slow 3-second downstream ≈ 120 in flight — suddenly connection pools and memory matter. This is why latency degradation cascades into resource exhaustion.

## Common mistakes

- Adding app pods when the database is the bottleneck (it gets worse)
- Scaling a stateful service horizontally and losing sessions/connections
- Reasoning from average latency while p99 users suffer
- Quoting throughput as if it proved good latency

## Key terms

- Stateless — any instance can serve any request; the scaling precondition
- Horizontal vs vertical — more machines vs bigger machine
- p95 / p99 — tail latency; what you set objectives on
- Bottleneck — find it by measuring before scaling anything
- Concurrency ≈ rps × latency — the back-of-envelope formula
- Peak vs average — size for the spike, not the mean

questions.title: Outbox pattern and the dual write
questions.prompt: A service saves an order to its database and publishes an event to a queue. What can go wrong, and how does the outbox pattern fix it?
questions.answerStructureHint: Name the dual-write problem, write the event into an outbox table in the same transaction, relay it, and handle at-least-once on consumers.

expectedPoints.label: Names the dual-write problem
expectedPoints.description: DB commit and publish are two systems — one can succeed while the other fails, losing or ghosting events.

expectedPoints.label: Outbox row in the same transaction
expectedPoints.description: The event is inserted into an outbox table atomically with the business change; a relay publishes it later.

expectedPoints.label: Consumers handle at-least-once
expectedPoints.description: The relay may publish twice, so consumers deduplicate / are idempotent.

followUps.prompt: Does the outbox guarantee event ordering? What would you do if order matters?

questions.title: Sharding and hot partitions
questions.prompt: Your database no longer fits on one node, so you shard it. How do you pick the partition key, and what is a hot shard?
questions.answerStructureHint: Key must spread load evenly and match query patterns; hot shards come from skewed keys; mitigation via key salting or splitting.

expectedPoints.label: Partition key drives distribution and queries
expectedPoints.description: Pick a high-cardinality key that spreads writes and keeps common queries on one shard.

expectedPoints.label: Explains hot shards
expectedPoints.description: Skewed access (one big tenant, celebrity user, time-ordered keys) overloads one shard while others idle.

expectedPoints.label: Knows mitigations and costs
expectedPoints.description: Salting/composite keys, splitting shards, caching hot data; cross-shard transactions and rebalancing get hard.

followUps.prompt: You need to double the shard count on a live system. How do you migrate without downtime?

questions.title: Read replicas and replication lag
questions.prompt: You add read replicas to take load off the primary database. What new problems did you just introduce?
questions.answerStructureHint: Replication lag → stale reads and read-your-own-writes; routing decisions; failover consistency.

expectedPoints.label: Replication lag causes stale reads
expectedPoints.description: Replicas apply changes asynchronously, so reads can return old data.

expectedPoints.label: Read-your-own-writes problem
expectedPoints.description: A user updates then reads a replica and sees the old value; route their reads to the primary or use session stickiness.
acceptedSignals: sees the old value after saving
read from the leader after write

expectedPoints.label: Failover and lost writes
expectedPoints.description: Promoting a lagging replica on failover can lose recent writes; sync vs async is a durability/latency trade-off.

followUps.prompt: A user saves their profile and immediately sees the old name. What happened and how do you fix it?

questions.title: Optimistic locking and N+1
questions.prompt: Two users edit the same record at once. How does optimistic locking handle it — and separately, what is the N+1 query problem?
questions.answerStructureHint: Version column checked on update, conflict → retry/merge; N+1: one query per row from lazy loading, fixed with joins/batching.

expectedPoints.label: Version check on update
expectedPoints.description: Update carries the version it read; if the row changed meanwhile, the update fails instead of silently overwriting.

expectedPoints.label: Handles the conflict
expectedPoints.description: On conflict: reload and retry, merge, or surface to the user — better than blocking for rare conflicts.

expectedPoints.label: Explains N+1 and the fix
expectedPoints.description: One query for the list plus one per item (lazy loading); fix with joins, eager loading or batching.

followUps.prompt: How would you notice an N+1 problem in production rather than in code review?

questions.title: Diagnosing a slow list endpoint
questions.prompt: A list endpoint backed by SQL got slow as data grew. Walk me through your diagnosis and the usual culprits.

expectedPoints.label: Index analysis first
expectedPoints.description: Check the query plan; index the filtered/sorted/joined columns; know indexes slow writes.

expectedPoints.label: Spots N+1
expectedPoints.description: One list query then per-row queries; fix with join/eager/batch loading.

expectedPoints.label: Connection pool math
expectedPoints.description: Pool too small queues requests; pools multiply across pods against one DB.

expectedPoints.label: Measures before scaling
expectedPoints.description: DB time, external calls, locks — find the bottleneck before adding servers.

questions.title: Introducing a cache without lying to users
questions.prompt: A dashboard aggregate is expensive and read constantly. How do you cache it, and how do you keep users from seeing wrong data?

expectedPoints.label: Cache-fit judgment
expectedPoints.description: Read pattern + consistency need decide; summaries cacheable, payment status fresh.

expectedPoints.label: Invalidation strategy
expectedPoints.description: TTL vs explicit delete on update vs write-through vs background refresh.

expectedPoints.label: Stampede awareness
expectedPoints.description: Hot key expiry → thundering herd; stagger TTLs or coalesce requests.

questions.title: Scale out or scale up?
questions.prompt: Traffic is growing. How do you decide between scaling vertically and horizontally, and what has to be true about the service for horizontal scaling to work?

expectedPoints.label: Horizontal vs vertical trade-off
expectedPoints.description: Bigger machine vs more pods; horizontal is the cloud default but has preconditions.

expectedPoints.label: Statelessness precondition
expectedPoints.description: Any pod must serve any request; state externalized (JWT, Redis); sticky state blocks scaling.

expectedPoints.label: Bottleneck first
expectedPoints.description: If the DB is the constraint, more pods make it worse; measure before scaling.

expectedPoints.label: Tail latency literacy
expectedPoints.description: p95/p99 over averages; throughput ≠ good latency.
